text: So we will discuss the laparoscopic splenectomy. Spleen is hidden within the rib cage unless until it is double in the size. So normal spleen cannot be palpable. It is within the costal margin. Your target is hilum. This is your target.

And according to that you have to draw two arcs. One is another is twenty-four. So, telescope should be ten centimeter lateral in twenty-four. Two umbilicus, one port will be here and one port will be here.

The surgeon identifies and visually examines the spleen's surrounding ligaments, including the gastroesplenic, phrenicoesplenic, splenocolic, and phrenicocolic ligaments, noting their positions and supportive roles.

There are different approach of doing splenectomy like anterior approach, lateral approach, inferior approach. French people they do lateral approach. Americans generally they do anterior approach and inferior approach. Indication, case selection is important. Although you can do any explain, but most of the autoimmune diseases are better to deal with the laparoscopy. Like ITP, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, microspherocytosis, benign tumor and cyst and HIV related thrombocytopenia. These are the best indication.

Hematological malignancy and moderate splenomegaly is a contraindication. But relative contraindication. And massive splenomegaly and portal hypertension is an absolute contraindication. Because in massive splenomegaly you may not have that much space to put your trocar. like if the patient has Kalajar or others, in this massive esophagus, sometimes it is up to the umbilicus. You cannot do laparoscopy.

The patient is positioned in a semi-lateral position with the left side elevated. The arms are supported, and the surgical team is arranged with the surgeon on the ventral aspect, an assistant on the dorsal aspect, and the scrub nurse nearby. The monitor is positioned near the shoulder, with an option for a second monitor for the assistant. The costal margin and iliac crest are visible, and the patient is draped accordingly.

The surgeon positions the ports for the splenectomy, placing them ten centimeters lateral to the umbilicus along the mid-clavicular line. The location of the spleen's hilum is assessed to determine the appropriate arc for port placement, adjusting for spleen size. If the hilum is too low, it is considered a contraindication for the procedure.

The surgeon positions the varus needle perpendicularly for access, followed by irrigation, suction, and the hanging drop test before starting insufflation. A disposable trocar is introduced in one go, and the telescope is inserted to visualize the splenic hilum and establish the arc.

The video shows the costal margin and the hilum of the spleen, with the surgical arc being established. Two ports are positioned contralaterally to achieve a sixty-degree angle for optimal access to the hilum.

And this is a shape of a diamond. Now if you want additional port, you can put one port, seven point five centimeter lateral here. This will be lever retractor. And if you want one other port, you can put it here. So this way all the port will be in the same arc. This is a gene and this is twenty-four. Telescope is twenty-four centimeter away so that you can have the better mobilization and you can see the panoramic view also. So this is the way how the axis will be performed. Here is the costal margin. This is the umbilicus, so it should be ten centimeter lateral. This is iliac crest and the hilum will be here. So one port will be here, one port will be here. This is your port position. Telescope will go like that. If you want additional port, you can put it here. This is the port position.

The surgical team positions the telescope ten centimeters lateral to the umbilicus, marking the initial port position. The arc and target area for the splenectomy are identified. The surgeon begins by mobilizing the inferior pole of the spleen.

The surgeon identifies the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments connecting the colon to the spleen. These ligaments are freed to allow retraction of the spleen, making the hilum more visible. The inferior edge of the spleen is lifted, and the short gastric vessels are dissected using a stapler or ligature harmonic. The hilum is then dissected, clipped, or stapled to separate it from surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses a hook to separate the inferior pole of the spleen from the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments. The lower pole is lifted up, demonstrating the first step of the splenectomy procedure in a spherocytosis case.

The surgeon frees the splenocolic and phrenocolic ligaments, preparing to dissect the short gastric vessels. The hilum is then approached for dissection, typically using a vascular stapler for ligation.

Only one percent is doing by the clip or suture. If you want to be your life easy, then you should do by the stapler. If someone is using the stapler for appendicectomy, I am not in the favor. You should do by the knot. But for splenectomy, use of a stapler is better. Because if you want to do with the knot or with the clip, you have to do a lot of dissection. You need to make a window between the artery and vein. You have to do the perihilar entire fat to nicely dissect. But with the stapler, a lot of your energy will be saved. Directly you can fire and you can do it much easier and much short time. White color cartridge has to be used. twelve millimeter port is required. So you have to enlarge the incision to make twelve millimeter and then port will be introduced. This is endo-GI linear stapler. You can use tri-stapler. You can use Aslon-sixty also by the Corvidian. And you will use white color cartridge as you know that In this stapler, if you will pull this back, the jaw opens. And if you close this one, jaw closes. Before firing, you have to press this button.

After that, you can fire. So, different color coding is B for bubble, G for green in the muscle, and W, white for vessels. White can be used as a universal. Some people, they use for bubble also, the white. because white doesn't have a predefined bending point. You know it is very important to learn that every stapler has predefined bending point. Like suppose this is a stapler, then they have a, this is a bending point. Company is hammering it here. And why it is hammered? Because when you press it, it bends from the bending point. Am I clear? It will bend. You will take a rod and you hammer the rod at one place and then bend it, it will bend from where? Where it is hammered. This bending point. But in the vascular staplers, the bending point is here. at this corner because if they will define the bending point it will bleed. Let us take one example if your stapler is made for fifteen staples and if you are stapling fifty then it will make a C. You are stapling fifty pages then it will make a shape of a C. And if it is made for fifty pages and you are stapling only two pages, then what will happen? It will be loose. It will be loose, there will be gap between the papers. And if it is a predefined staple line, vessels with the blood look eight mm. But after excusing the blood out, it looks two mm or three mm. So how can you decide the thickness of the vessel? So that's why white cartridge, when you fire the staple, It bends like that. It doesn't make a shape of a bee. Because making the shape of a bee is risky. If it is thick, it will make a shape of a C. And if it is thin, then this bee will be loose. And from that much loose space, bleeding will happen. That much loose space, blood will flow. So that's why white color is universal.

The surgeon loads a white cartridge onto the stapler, specifically designed for vessels, and rotates it clockwise to lock it in place. The jaw of the stapler is opened by pulling back, and closed by pressing a button, preparing for firing.

The surgeon uses a stapler with a white cartridge to fire staples across the splenic hilum, creating three rows of staples on either side and cutting in between. The staple line is visible, with slight oozing that is managed with titanium clips. The procedure progresses towards the esplenoprenic ligament, with remaining fibers of the frenico-esplenic ligament detached using a hook, completing the splenectomy.

The surgeon uses a stapler to perform the splenectomy, loading a cartridge and inserting it through a 12mm cannula. The left hand grasper positions the pedicle between the stapler jaws, ensuring even distribution before firing. The stapler fires, advancing 15mm, and then the blade retracts, completing the staple line.

The surgeon uses a size D endo bag with a metal ring and purse string to retrieve the spleen. The metal ring detaches from the plastic when the purse string is pulled, allowing the solid organ to be securely enclosed and extracted. The base of the size D endo bag is flat, accommodating the spleen or liver, unlike the conical base of size A and B endo bags.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of a metal ring endo bag, inserting it through a 12mm port. The piston mechanism ejects the metal ring, which automatically encapsulates the spleen within the bag. The strong metal ring ensures secure containment, unlike gloves or polypropylene, and pulling the string detaches the ring from the plastic.

The surgeon uses a purse string to close the mouth of the endo bag containing a smaller spleen. The metal ring is detached and removed separately. The spleen is morselated using ovum forceps within the endo bag, avoiding electrical morselation to prevent spleenosis. The morselated spleen is extracted, and a drain is placed before closing the wound with skin stapler, glue, or suture.

The surgeon closes the port at the end of the splenectomy procedure, injecting xylocaine to prevent post-operative pain. An ester strip is applied to the incision site. The video transitions to a partial splenectomy, showing the use of a stapler with purple cartridges to manipulate tissue and apply three rows of staples. The stapler is initially used as a simple grasper during the procedure.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to hold and retract the stomach, positioning the endogealine between the jaws of the stapler. The stapler is then fired, cutting and stapling the tissue with three rows of staples on either side. The device features variable staple heights and a sharp blade that cuts between the cartridge jaw and the face jaw, which has grooves to bend the staples.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of a tri-stapler, highlighting the loaded blade in the cartridge and the variable height staples. The outer staples are for stapling, while the inner ones ensure hemostasis, preventing bleeding. The lower jaw of the stapler is shown, with three rows of staples on either side. The sharp blade cuts between the staples with each firing, advancing 15 mm per cut. The cartridge allows for partial use, such as a 30 mm cut from a 60 mm cartridge.

The surgeon uses the face jaw with a groove to bend the staples, applying Gore-Tex tape over both the face jaw and cartridge jaw to prevent bleeding when firing staples on the spleen. The tape provides compression and strength, ensuring effective hemostasis during the procedure.

The surgeon demonstrates the separable cartridge mechanism, showing how to open and lock the cartridge with a simple press. The cartridge is blue, and the face jaw has a groove for bending. Gore-Tex tape is applied on either side of the jaws for use in partial splenectomy. The instrument is articulated, allowing it to bend up to sixty degrees and return to a straight position.

And when you have to make it a straight, pull this one, it is a straight. This one, this knob, you have to pull towards you. Then you can bend. And leave it, it is locked. And if you have to make it a straight, pull again towards you. And become a straight. So this is very useful. And the same is true for... So... So... You have to remove the cartridge, and this is the tri-stapler. Tri-stapler, how to attach? It is having inbuilt the blade. And there is an arrow here. This arrow. And there is one arrow on the gun. Here is another arrow. And both the arrows should be towards each other. See, and then it will enter. and then just you rotate it clockwise direction and it is locked. Here it is locked. It is done. And then again here how to close? This is to close, this is to open. This is to close and this is to open. And to articulate you have this knob and it can articulate. like this, and this is to articulate other side. So this is tri-stapler. And before firing, you have to press this knob. Once you press this knob, you can fire it.

The surgeon applies Gore-Tex tape to the spleen using a tri-stapler, performing a partial splenectomy by firing the stapler to secure the tape and cut the tissue. The cut spleen is placed into a size D endo bag for removal. Suction irrigation is used to manage minor oozing, and flow seal is applied to the surgical site to prevent further bleeding.

The surgeon applies a hemostatic matrix containing gelatin to the surgical site, which initiates the coagulation cascade to stop bleeding. The matrix solidifies quickly upon application, effectively controlling oozing but not active bleeding.

The surgeon uses a tripolar device to perform a splenectomy, holding and coagulating the short gastric vessels before cutting them with the inbuilt blade. The tripolar is attached to a force triad high-frequency electrosurgical machine, facilitating precise coagulation and cutting.

The surgeon separates the esplenocolic and frenicocolic ligaments from the spleen using a tripolar instrument, which functions as a blunt and sharp dissector, as well as a bipolar coagulator. Traction is applied to form a clear dissection plane, while the Maryland retractor prevents accidental piercing of the sling. Care is taken to avoid collateral damage to the splenic flexure of the colon and the lower pole of the spleen.
The surgeon carefully skeletonizes the splenic artery and vein, preparing them for sealing using energy sources. This meticulous process requires patience to ensure complete isolation of the vessels.

The surgeon identifies the tail of the pancreas near the surgical field and highlights the importance of careful dissection around the short gastric vessels and the greater curvature of the stomach. The hilum is visible, and the surrounding fat is being skeletonized to ensure safe stapling, especially when using an endogia stapler.

The surgeon separates the remaining fibers of the lower pole of the spleen from the colon, allowing the spleen to be lifted and freed. There is a minor burn on the peritoneum of the abdominal wall due to overshooting with energy devices, which is safe compared to potential bowel perforation. The pancreas is visible in the surgical field.

The pancreas is visible and must be handled carefully. The lower pole of the spleen is completely free, allowing the hilum to be pulled over the surface. The surgeon begins to skeletonize the hilum, with the artery positioned posteriorly and the vein anteriorly, ensuring precise dissection near the hilum.

The surgeon removes peri-hilar fat to create a window between the splenic artery and vein. The lower pole of the spleen is lifted and mobilized. The surgeon coagulates at two places before cutting between them, using intermittent activation of the device, ensuring not to press for more than three seconds at a time. The artery and vein are clearly visible.

The surgeon identifies the splenic artery and vein, creating a window between them. Using bipolar harmonic, the artery is coagulated at two places before cutting in between. The blade is visible as it cuts through the coagulated section.

The surgeon uses a blade to coagulate and cut the vein at two places, demonstrating the use of energy for splenectomy. The pulsating splenic artery is visible, successfully coagulated with energy, though this technique is generally avoided due to risks. The hilum is completed, and the remaining short gastric vessels are cut using an inferior and anterior approach.

The surgeon addresses the remaining short gastric vessels during the splenectomy procedure. The lateral approach is mentioned as an alternative technique, which involves complete mobilization of the spleen before addressing the hilum.
The surgeon demonstrates the lateral approach for splenectomy, with the port positions remaining unchanged. The left and right hands are visible, targeting the area with minor adhesions present.

The surgeon begins by mobilizing the adhesions around the spleen. Using the French lateral approach, the spleen is progressively mobilized dorsally towards the diaphragm, shifting its position from the initial location.

The surgeon mobilizes the spleen using the lateral approach, flipping it over the stomach. The spleen is positioned over the stomach with the hilum visible, and the left lobe of the liver is adjacent to the spleen.

The surgeon shows the hilum of the spleen, which has been completely flipped over the stomach. A gauze piece separates the structures, and the artery is visible anteriorly. The vascular pedicle is being esculinized using a hook. The diaphragm, stomach, fundus, and hilum are clearly visible, along with the phrenico-splenic ligament being cut. The spleen is fully mobilized, with the last fiber from the diaphragm freed.

The surgeon creates a window in the splenic artery and passes a suture to lift it, using an endo-GIA stapler with 30 mm cartridges to staple the artery and vein separately. The left lobe of the liver, stomach, and splenic fossa are visible. The vein is encircled with a suture and lifted before being stapled. The dorsal aspect of the spleen lifts the liver, and the spleen is mobilized, crossing the falciform ligament and nearing the gallbladder. The short gastric vessels are freed, and the spleen is prepared for removal.
